segments.title: Graf Zeppelin air group lacked torpedo depth
segments.summary: The Graf Zeppelin aircraft discussion argues that Germany lacked the aerial torpedoes and production depth needed for a credible carrier strike group.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke treats the Fi 167 and Ju 87C as symptoms of a larger naval-aviation problem rather than isolated aircraft choices. The carrier needed an air group ready before trials, but Germany had built only a small number of Fi 167s and had weak aerial-torpedo production and reliability in 1939. That gap made a nominal torpedo-bomber force look risky: the aircraft might fly, but the weapons and training base behind them did not support a serious carrier striking capability.

evidence.note: The transcript explains that Graf Zeppelin needed aircraft in production and an air wing ready before carrier service, but Fi 167 output was too small for training and accident losses.
The torpedo section says Germany produced very few aerial torpedoes in 1939, had high failure rates, and remained short of reliable torpedoes for much of the war.
